title: Listening - Part 3: Memorable Journeys
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a journey they particularly enjoyed. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the reason each speaker gives for enjoying the journey. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a journey they particularly enjoyed. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the reason each speaker gives for enjoying the journey. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about a journey they particularly enjoyed.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: Last autumn I took a ferry along the west coast of Scotland rather than flying to the island where my friend lives. It certainly wasn't quick, and the café on board was nothing special. [19]But we passed dark cliffs, tiny islands and stretches of sea lit by the late afternoon sun; I spent nearly the whole crossing outside watching it all.[/19] By the time we arrived, I felt the holiday had already started.||Speaker 2 Man: During my train holiday in Italy, engineering work meant we had to get off unexpectedly at a small station and wait several hours for another service. I was annoyed at first. Then the station clerk told us about a nearby market. [20]It wasn't anywhere in our guidebook, but we walked there, had an excellent lunch and explored a lovely town we would otherwise never have seen.[/20] The delay became my favourite part of the trip.||Speaker 3 Woman: I had to take an overnight coach across the country on my own, and friends warned me that it might be unpleasant. In fact, the driver explained every stop clearly, checked that everyone returned before leaving, and drove very steadily even in heavy rain. [21]I had expected to feel anxious on such a long night journey, but I was comfortable and secure from beginning to end.[/21] I even managed to sleep for a few hours.||Speaker 4 Man: My older brother and I cycled along a riverside route over three days. Neither of us is particularly fast, so we stopped often and didn't cover a great distance. [22]What made it special was that, with no one else around and no screens to distract us, we talked properly for the first time in ages.[/22] We discussed university, our parents and plans for next year. The cycling itself was almost secondary.||Speaker 5 Woman: People were surprised when I decided to travel from London to Vienna by train because it took most of the day. Usually I'm rushing from work to social plans, and the novel on my bedside table never gets finished. [23]On that journey there was nothing I needed to do, so I settled into my seat and read nearly an entire book without interruption.[/23] For me, that was a rare luxury, and much better than flying.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: Last autumn I took a ferry along the west coast of Scotland rather than flying to the island where my friend lives. It certainly wasn't quick, and the café on board was nothing special. But we passed dark cliffs, tiny islands and stretches of sea lit by the late afternoon sun; I spent nearly the whole crossing outside watching it all. By the time we arrived, I felt the holiday had already started.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: During my train holiday in Italy, engineering work meant we had to get off unexpectedly at a small station and wait several hours for another service. I was annoyed at first. Then the station clerk told us about a nearby market. It wasn't anywhere in our guidebook, but we walked there, had an excellent lunch and explored a lovely town we would otherwise never have seen. The delay became my favourite part of the trip.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: I had to take an overnight coach across the country on my own, and friends warned me that it might be unpleasant. In fact, the driver explained every stop clearly, checked that everyone returned before leaving, and drove very steadily even in heavy rain. I had expected to feel anxious on such a long night journey, but I was comfortable and secure from beginning to end. I even managed to sleep for a few hours.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: My older brother and I cycled along a riverside route over three days. Neither of us is particularly fast, so we stopped often and didn't cover a great distance. What made it special was that, with no one else around and no screens to distract us, we talked properly for the first time in ages. We discussed university, our parents and plans for next year. The cycling itself was almost secondary.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: People were surprised when I decided to travel from London to Vienna by train because it took most of the day. Usually I'm rushing from work to social plans, and the novel on my bedside table never gets finished. On that journey there was nothing I needed to do, so I settled into my seat and read nearly an entire book without interruption. For me, that was a rare luxury, and much better than flying.

options.text: discovering a place they had not planned to visit
having time to talk to a family member
being able to read for a long time
the impressive views on the way

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker particularly enjoyed looking at the coastal scenery from the ferry.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: A delay led the speaker to enjoy visiting an unplanned town.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker was pleased by how secure she felt during an overnight coach journey.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker valued having uninterrupted time to talk to his brother.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The long train journey gave the speaker the uninterrupted reading time she wanted.
